questions.question: Incitement is a common law inchoate offence. What is the core definition of criminal incitement?

options.A: Where a person encourages, persuades, or commands another to commit a criminal act — the offence is complete only when the incited person takes a step in furtherance of the incited crime
options.B: Where a person by words, conduct, or other communication, urges, solicits, or encourages another to commit a criminal act — the offence is complete upon the communication reaching the incited person; it is not necessary that the incited person agrees to, attempts, or completes the incited offence
options.C: Where a person commands another to commit a crime and that other person carries out the crime — incitement requires the incited offence to be completed
options.D: Where a person expresses a desire that a crime be committed — incitement can be established by a private, uncommunicated wish to see an offence committed

questions.explanation: Criminal incitement is the common law inchoate offence of urging, soliciting, or encouraging another person to commit a criminal act. The essential features are: (1) there must be a communication that reaches the incited person (or in some cases the public) — a purely private, uncommunicated desire does not constitute incitement; (2) the offence is complete upon the communication reaching the target — the incited person does not have to agree to carry out the crime, attempt it, or complete it; (3) the mens rea is an intention that the incited person will commit the offence. Incitement is one of the three main inchoate offences (alongside conspiracy and attempt), and like the others, it criminalises conduct before any harm has been caused by the substantive offence. Option A incorrectly adds a requirement of a step in furtherance by the incited person. Option C incorrectly requires the incited offence to be completed. Option D incorrectly includes uncommunicated private wishes and thereby removes the actus reus requirement.

questions.question: In AG v Capaldi [1955] (Ireland), the accused offered a Garda money to allow a vehicle to pass a checkpoint without inspection. The court considered whether this constituted incitement. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held no offence was committed — an offer of money is merely an expression of desire, which does not rise to the level of criminal incitement
options.B: The court held that the offer of money constituted incitement — the offer was a direct and active solicitation to the Garda to commit an offence (accepting a bribe and facilitating unlawful conduct); it went beyond a mere wish or expression and constituted an attempt to move the Garda to action
options.C: The court held that the conduct constituted bribery only, not incitement — the two offences are mutually exclusive
options.D: The court held that incitement requires verbal communication only — an offer of money (a physical act) cannot constitute incitement

questions.explanation: AG v Capaldi [1955] is an important Irish authority on what constitutes sufficient encouragement for incitement. The court held that the accused's offer of money to the Garda was a direct solicitation — an active attempt to move the officer to commit an offence (accepting a corrupt payment and failing in his duty). The case establishes that incitement does not require verbal instruction or commands — it can take the form of an offer, inducement, or temptation. What matters is that the accused is actively trying to cause the other person to commit an offence, rather than merely expressing a passive wish that it would happen. The offer of a financial reward is a paradigm example of actively soliciting criminal conduct and squarely within the definition of incitement. Option A incorrectly characterises the offer as a mere expression. Option C incorrectly creates mutual exclusivity between bribery and incitement. Option D incorrectly limits incitement to verbal communication.

questions.question: In R v Higgins [1801] (England and Wales), the accused expressed a desire that a servant would rob their master. The court considered whether this constituted incitement. What principle did this case establish?

options.A: The case established that any expression of a desire that a crime be committed constitutes incitement — the mental element of incitement is satisfied by a wish
options.B: The case established that a mere expression of desire or wish that a crime be committed is insufficient for incitement — the accused must actively solicit, urge, or encourage the other person to commit the offence; passive expression falls short of the actus reus of incitement
options.C: The case established that incitement requires the accused to provide material assistance to the incited person — words alone are never sufficient
options.D: The case established that incitement only applies to offences against property — violent offences are outside the scope of the doctrine

questions.explanation: R v Higgins [1801] is foundational authority on the distinction between active incitement and passive expression. The court held that simply expressing a wish or desire that a crime be committed is not sufficient to constitute incitement. The actus reus of incitement requires that the accused actively urge, solicit, encourage, or press the other person to commit the offence. There must be an active attempt to move the other person to criminal conduct — a mere statement of desire or hope is not enough. This principle ensures that incitement captures genuine attempts to bring about crime rather than idle conversation. The Higgins principle has been consistently applied: the solicitation must be genuine and active, not merely expressive. Option A incorrectly treats passive expression as sufficient. Option C goes too far in requiring material assistance — words are sufficient if they constitute genuine solicitation. Option D incorrectly limits incitement to property offences.

questions.question: In R v Goldman [2001] CA (England and Wales), the accused sent unsolicited paedophilic material through the post to persons he hoped would be interested. The Court of Appeal considered whether this constituted incitement even though the recipients had not requested the material and there was no evidence the material had any influence on them. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held that sending unsolicited material cannot constitute incitement because the recipients had no prior relationship with the accused and had not expressed any interest
options.B: The court held that sending the material did constitute incitement — it was an active communication encouraging the recipients to engage in the criminal acts depicted; incitement does not require that the incited person be influenced by or respond to the communication; it is complete upon the communication reaching the other person
options.C: The court held that incitement requires a response from the incited person — where there was no evidence of any effect on the recipients, incitement was not established
options.D: The court held that incitement through the postal system requires a separate statutory offence and cannot be prosecuted at common law

questions.explanation: R v Goldman [2001] CA is a significant case establishing that incitement does not require the incited person to be influenced by, respond to, or act upon the communication. The court held that sending unsolicited paedophilic material through the post constituted incitement because: (1) the material was a communication actively encouraging recipients to engage in criminal acts; (2) the offence was complete when the material reached the recipients — no further response or influence was required; (3) the fact that the communication was unsolicited and uninvited did not prevent it from constituting incitement. This case confirms that the actus reus of incitement focuses on the sending of the communication, not its effect on the recipient. Option A incorrectly requires a prior relationship or expressed interest. Option C incorrectly requires a response from the incited person. Option D incorrectly requires a separate statutory offence for postal communications.

questions.question: In R v Most [1881] (England and Wales), the accused published an article in a newspaper inciting the assassination of heads of state. The court considered whether incitement could be committed through mass publication where no specific individual was targeted. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held that incitement requires a specific named individual to be incited — mass publication to an anonymous public cannot constitute incitement
options.B: The court held that publication of material inciting criminal conduct can constitute incitement even where no specific individual is targeted — it is sufficient that the material reaches the public and is capable of inciting members of that public to commit the offence; incitement need not be directed at one identifiable person
options.C: The court held that incitement through publication is a separate offence from common law incitement and requires proof of an actual reader who was influenced
options.D: The court held that publishing incitement to violence against public officials was protected expression and not criminal incitement

questions.explanation: R v Most [1881] established that incitement can be committed by mass publication — it is not limited to direct person-to-person communications targeting a specific individual. The court held that publishing material urging the assassination of heads of state constituted incitement because: (1) the material was capable of inciting members of the public who read it to commit the offence; (2) incitement is complete when the material reaches the audience — no specific individual needs to be identified or influenced; (3) the incitement offence captures the accused's attempt to bring about criminal conduct through any means of communication, including publication. Most confirms that the breadth of the communication (to an entire readership rather than one person) does not limit the scope of incitement. Option A incorrectly requires a specific identifiable individual. Option C incorrectly creates a separate offence for publication. Option D incorrectly characterises the material as protected expression.

questions.question: In DPP v Callan [2009] IECCA (Ireland), the court considered what is required for the actus reus of incitement in Irish law. What principle did the court apply?

options.A: The court held that incitement in Irish law requires proof that the incited person actually committed or attempted the incited offence
options.B: The court confirmed that incitement requires an overt act of solicitation — the accused must actively communicate with and attempt to move the other person to commit the offence; passive awareness of a planned crime or mere failure to prevent it does not constitute incitement; the communication must be directed at moving the other person to criminal action
options.C: The court held that incitement can be established by circumstantial evidence of an implied agreement between the accused and the incited person
options.D: The court held that incitement in Irish law requires the accused to have a financial motive for wanting the incited offence committed

questions.explanation: DPP v Callan [2009] IECCA confirmed the Irish law position on the actus reus of incitement. The court held that incitement requires an overt act of solicitation — the accused must actively communicate with and attempt to move another person to commit a criminal offence. Passive conduct — such as knowing about a planned offence and doing nothing — is insufficient. The actus reus requires active encouragement, urging, or solicitation directed at causing the other person to act criminally. This is consistent with R v Higgins and the broader common law position: incitement is about actively trying to bring about criminal conduct, not merely knowing of or failing to prevent it. The Callan decision is important for Irish FE-1 candidates as it confirms the domestic application of the core incitement principles. Option A incorrectly adds a requirement of actual commission or attempt by the incited person. Option C incorrectly introduces an implied agreement test (more characteristic of conspiracy). Option D incorrectly requires a financial motive.

questions.question: In R v Whitehouse [1977] CA (England and Wales), a father incited his 15-year-old daughter to engage in sexual intercourse with him. Due to her age, the daughter could not legally consent and therefore could not be guilty of incest herself. The court considered whether the father committed incitement even though the incited person could not have been convicted of the incited offence. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held there was no incitement because a person cannot be incited to commit an offence of which they are legally incapable — the daughter's incapacity to be guilty negated the father's incitement
options.B: The court held that the father was guilty of incitement — the relevant question is whether the act incited would have constituted a criminal offence if committed by a person capable of committing it; where the act incited would be a crime if done by someone with capacity, the inciter is guilty even if the specific person incited lacks the capacity to commit the offence
options.C: The court held that the father was guilty only of attempt — where incitement cannot succeed because the incited person lacks capacity, the charge must be reduced to attempt
options.D: The court held that the father was not guilty of incitement but was guilty of the substantive offence through the doctrine of innocent agency

questions.explanation: R v Whitehouse [1977] CA (not to be confused with R v Whitehouse [1941] on withdrawal) established an important principle on incitement and the capacity of the incited person. The court held that the father was guilty of incitement notwithstanding that the daughter, at 15, could not legally be guilty of incest. The relevant question for incitement is whether the act incited would constitute a criminal offence if committed by a person capable of committing it — not whether the specific individual targeted was capable of being convicted. Since incest is an offence that an adult could commit, the father's solicitation of that conduct amounted to incitement. This prevents persons from using the legal incapacity of the target as a shield against incitement liability. Note: this Whitehouse case is distinct from R v Whitehouse [1941] which dealt with withdrawal from common design. Option A incorrectly makes the target's capacity determinative. Option C incorrectly reduces the charge to attempt. Option D incorrectly characterises the situation as innocent agency (the daughter was not the accused's instrument in the same sense).

questions.question: Adrian sends a letter to Brenda urging her to commit arson at a competitor's premises. Brenda reads the letter and dismisses it — she has no intention of committing any offence and throws the letter away. Has Adrian committed incitement?

options.A: No — incitement requires that the incited person at least consider acting on the communication; since Brenda dismissed it immediately, the offence is not complete
options.B: Yes — Adrian's incitement is complete upon the letter reaching Brenda; the fact that Brenda did not respond to, agree with, or act upon the incitement is irrelevant — the offence is complete when the communication reaches the incited person
options.C: No — incitement of arson requires a verbal or in-person communication; a written letter cannot constitute incitement of a violent or destructive offence
options.D: Yes — but only if it can be proved that the letter caused Brenda some measurable distress or anxiety, thereby establishing the necessary effect of the communication

questions.explanation: Adrian has committed incitement. Applying R v Goldman [2001] and the general principles of incitement law: the offence is complete when the communication reaches the incited person. It is immaterial that Brenda: (1) did not agree to commit arson; (2) had no intention of acting on the letter; (3) dismissed the letter immediately; or (4) experienced no influence from the communication. The actus reus of incitement — an active communication urging another to commit a crime — is complete upon receipt. Adrian sent a communication actively urging Brenda to commit arson (a criminal offence), and that communication reached her. Adrian is guilty of incitement. The failure of the incitement (Brenda's refusal to act) does not retroactively eliminate the completed offence. This is precisely why incitement is characterised as an inchoate offence — it criminalises the attempt to bring about crime without requiring the crime to be completed. Options A and D incorrectly add requirements regarding the effect on the incited person. Option C incorrectly limits incitement to verbal in-person communications.

questions.question: What is the mens rea required for the common law offence of criminal incitement?

options.A: The accused must have intended to communicate with the other person, but need not have intended that the other person would actually commit the incited offence — recklessness as to whether the communication would encourage crime is sufficient
options.B: The accused must have intended: (1) to communicate the incitement to the other person; and (2) that the other person would, as a result, commit the incited offence; mere recklessness or knowledge that the communication might encourage crime is insufficient — the accused must have intended the incited offence to be committed
options.C: Incitement is a strict liability offence — the prosecution need only prove the communication was made; no mental element is required
options.D: The accused must have intended both the communication and must have known with certainty that the incited person would commit the offence — a mere hope or desire that the person would act is insufficient

questions.explanation: The mens rea for criminal incitement has two components: (1) the accused must have intended to make the communication — incitement cannot be committed accidentally or recklessly; and (2) the accused must have intended that the incited person would, as a result of the communication, go on to commit the incited offence. This second element is the key distinguishing feature: the accused must be trying to bring about the commission of the offence, not merely conveying information or expressing a view that might incidentally encourage crime. Recklessness as to whether the communication would cause the other person to commit a crime is insufficient — the accused must intend this outcome. However, the mens rea does not require certainty that the incited person will act — it requires that the accused intended them to. Option A incorrectly accepts recklessness as to whether crime would be encouraged. Option C removes the mental element entirely. Option D incorrectly requires the accused to know with certainty that the incited person will commit the offence — intention that they will do so is sufficient, without certainty as to their response.
